publications.title: Structural dissimilarity from self drives neoepitope escape from immune tolerance.
publications.authors: Devlin JR, Alonso JA, Ayres CM, Keller GLJ, Bobisse S, Vander Kooi CW, Coukos G, Gfeller D, Harari A, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined how a single-point mutation in an ovarian cancer neoepitope alters peptide structure and dynamics to escape self-tolerance, using structural analysis of the neoepitope-specific TCR bound to its cognate pMHC-I. The mutation, positioned outside the MHC-binding anchor residues, reorganizes the peptide backbone into a conformation that optimally engages the TCR, enabling high-affinity binding and robust T-cell activation despite the neoepitope's derivation from a self-peptide precursor. This finding reveals a molecular mechanism by which structural divergence from self overcomes immune tolerance to neoepitopes, explaining why sequence homology alone cannot predict immunogenicity. The results have direct implications for neoantigen-based cancer immunotherapy and personalized vaccine design, highlighting the need to consider three-dimensional conformational changes rather than sequence similarity when identifying and engineering therapeutic neoepitopes.
